retrieved_docs.title: Ablative Laser Resurfacing -- Equipment -- Laser-safe Instruments
retrieved_docs.content: Fire extinguisher Wet towels Water or saline

retrieved_docs.title: Laser Tattoo Removal -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In contrast to the ruby, alexandrite, KTP, and Nd:YAG lasers, which are considered non-ablative, some authors advocate the use of fractionated ablative lasers, such as carbon dioxide and erbium-doped yttrium-aluminum-garnet (Er:YAG) lasers, to remove superficial pigment, like traumatic and amateur tattoos, either alone or in combination with non-ablative lasers. [27] [28] Ablative lasers vaporize the epidermis and superficial dermis by targeting water as their chromophore, thereby permitting the pigment particles to be cleared during the healing process. Ablative lasers have also shown promise for treating refractory tattoos, such as those with light-colored ink or those that have undergone paradoxical darkening. [29] These lasers are typically avoided for treating patients with dark skin, however, particularly if energy is delivered with a continuous wave rather than via a fractionated system.

retrieved_docs.title: Laser Carbon Dioxide Resurfacing -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Carbon dioxide refers to the lasing medium within the device, the part of the laser that produces the beam of collimated, coherent, and monochromatic light. The lasing medium of medical carbon dioxide lasers contains CO2, nitrogen, hydrogen, and helium gasses and produces infrared light with a peak wavelength of 10,600 nm. The chromophore - the molecule that predominantly absorbs the laser's energy - for this wavelength is water.

retrieved_docs.title: Ablative Laser Resurfacing -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The use of lasers for ablating and resurfacing is based upon the concept of selective thermolysis of the epidermal and dermal layers of the skin through the delivery of light energy. [1] [2] Light energy emitted by the laser is absorbed by the skin's two main chromophores, melanin, and water, which then emit thermal energy, destroying the surrounding tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: Laser Complications -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The process of targeting a specific chromophore is known as selective photothermolysis. The most common chromophores are water, tissue proteins, hemoglobin, and pigment (e.g., melanin or tattoo pigment). Lasers that target water and interstitial proteins tend to vaporize the tissue and are therefore termed "ablative." In contrast, lasers that target hemoglobin and other pigments tend not to cause tissue destruction directly and are therefore known as "non-ablative." Commonly employed ablative lasers are the carbon dioxide (CO2) and erbium-doped yttrium-aluminum-garnet (Er:YAG) lasers; examples of non-ablative lasers include pulsed dye (PDL), potassium-titanyl-phosphate (KTP), and neodymium-doped yttrium-aluminum-garnet (Nd:YAG) lasers.

retrieved_docs.title: Ablative Laser Resurfacing -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Er:YAG Ablative Lasers
retrieved_docs.content: The erbium-doped yttrium aluminum garnet laser was developed in the 1990s and produces laser light at a wavelength of 2,940 nm. The chromophore is water, similar to the CO2 laser; however, energy absorption is 12 to 18 times greater. Re-epithelialization of the epidermal layer occurs more rapidly when compared with CO2 lasers, in approximately five days and with erythema persisting only 3 to 4 weeks after treatment. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Ablative Laser Resurfacing -- Equipment -- Laser Selection
retrieved_docs.content: CO2 or Er:YAG lasers Fractional or non-fractional

retrieved_docs.title: Ablative Laser Resurfacing -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Laser Basics
retrieved_docs.content: A laser light emits photons at a particular wavelength, CO2 at 10,600 nm and Er:YAG at 2,940 nm, which will be absorbed by the target tissue's chromophores, namely water and melanin. Laser is an acronym that stands for light amplification by the stimulated emission of radiation; true laser light is collimated (all waves are parallel), coherent (all waves are in phase), and monochromatic (all waves have the same frequency and wavelength). The goal is to ensure adequate but safe depth of penetration of the laser and delivery of sufficient energy to the targeted tissues while minimizing heat transfer to adjacent structures. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Laser Erbium-Yag Resurfacing -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Er:YAG laser skin resurfacing is an effective treatment modality for a range of cutaneous indications, from spot treatment of lesions to full-field resurfacing. Emitting light at a peak wavelength of 2,940 nm, the laser's energy is absorbed by water in the epidermis and dermis. Epidermal ablation and resultant re-epithelialization can treat actinic damage, dyschromia, and coarse skin texture. Thermal injury in the dermis below the zone of ablation stimulates collagen production, reducing skin laxity and improving the appearance of static rhytids. With an appropriate selection of candidates for treatment with Er:YAG lasers, patients can achieve significantly improved skin appearance. Er:YAG laser facial skin resurfacing is most effective for patients with Fitzpatrick skin types I-II but can also be employed for Fitzpatrick skin types III-IV with careful laser pulse frequency and depth adjustment.

retrieved_docs.title: Ablative Laser Resurfacing -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Laser Basics
retrieved_docs.content: The depth of penetration of light energy is generally correlated with increasing light wavelengths. Carbon dioxide lasers generally produce a 20 to 60 μm depth of vaporization on the first pass compared with Er:YAG lasers that produce a 3 to 5 μm depth of vaporization on the first pass, depending on the settings used. [8] The advantage of CO2 and Er:YAG lasers is that the energy they emit is selectively absorbed by water in the epidermis, causing rapid vaporization of the epidermis and ensuring that little light energy reaches the deeper layers of the skin. Nonetheless, there is residual thermal damage caused by the penetration of energy, and this is believed to lead to collagen contraction, remodeling, and skin tightening. Thermal damage with traditional CO2 lasers and Er:YAG lasers may reach depths of 100-150 μm and 10-40 μm, respectively. [9] The choice of laser to employ often depends on a combination of clinician experience, patient factors, and device availability.

retrieved_docs.title: Airway for Laser Surgery -- Equipment -- Commonly Used Lasers
retrieved_docs.content: Carbon dioxide (CO2) lasers are historically the most commonly used lasers in otolaryngologic surgery. The infrared light produced is not visible to humans at 10,600 nm and produces intense thermal injury as its chromophore is water. [11] Use a visible-light aiming beam and ensure this aiming beam is accurate to the invisible laser before beginning the operation. Eyes (of both the patient and the operating room personnel) are especially vulnerable to the CO 2 laser, though simple, clear glass or plastic is sufficient for protection. [12] Specifications of these and other commonly used lasers (KTP, Ho:YAG, Nd:YAG, Argon, pulsed dye) are discussed elsewhere in StatPearls and are beyond the scope of this review.

retrieved_docs.title: Laser Treatment of Pigmented Lesions -- Technique or Treatment -- Treatment of Blue Lesions (Venous Malformations, Arteriovenous Malformations)
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment of Pigmented Nevi - Lasers commonly used in nevus removal can be either specific or nonspecific. Pigment nonspecific lasers target water as their chromophore and resurface the entire epidermis. They are outside the scope of this summary. [18] Specific lasers target melanosomes in the dermis, presumably lysing them photomechanically. Such lasers include the Q-switched ruby laser, the Q-switched alexandrite laser, and the Nd: YAG laser.  During the treatment of pigmented nevi with these lasers, the pulses must be shorter than the melanosomes' thermal relaxation time for lysis to occur. [14] Spot size and fluence can be varied, as with laser treatment of vascular anomalies. In general, deeper lesions are treated with larger spot sizes and higher fluence, as these settings improve penetration. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Airway for Laser Surgery -- Equipment -- Commonly Used Lasers
retrieved_docs.content: Personnel present in the room must wear wavelength-specific protective eyeglasses to prevent possible laser radiation exposure and irreparable corneal or retinal injury, and signage indicating laser use should be placed on the outer face of all doors to the operating room. In general, clear glass or plastic goggles are used when working with a CO 2 laser; amber or orange filters for KTP and argon lasers; green or clear filters for Nd:YAG; blue for dye lasers. [13] High-efficiency masks should be used to protect the staff from smoke plumes. These result from tissue vaporization and contain fine microparticles (mean size 0.3 micrometers) capable of depositing in the lower airways and alveoli, potentially posing risks of affecting mucociliary clearance, interstitial pneumonitis, bronchiolitis, and mutagenicity. This is of particular concern when treating papillomatous disease, as there is a hypothetical risk of seeding papilloma. [14] Plume scavenging systems should be employed because the commonly used general room ventilation (aka "dilution ventilation") is insufficient to remove air contaminants. Room suction hose nozzle inlet may be used as well and must be kept within two inches of the operative field to effectively evacuate airborne contaminants generated by laser devices. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Airway for Laser Surgery -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Every healthcare team member must take precautions during laser surgery of the upper airway to avoid harm to themself, the patient, or other operating room personnel. Wear proper protective equipment, which includes a high-efficiency mask and wavelength-specific protective eyeglasses. To decrease the risk of airway fire: Use the lowest concentration of oxygen possible Avoid using nitrous oxide as an anesthetic (at high temperatures, it can release oxygen and act as an oxidizer) Limit laser use to short pulses and the lowest power Activate the laser only when the tip of the laser source is visible, and the endotracheal tube is out of reach of the source. Cover the patient's face and chest with wet towels, and protect the eyes with wavelength-specific eye protection Have saline-filled large (eg, 50 mL) syringes within reach during procedures with a high risk of ignition Know the nearest location of a fire extinguisher and a water source

retrieved_docs.title: Laser Treatment of Pigmented Lesions -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Fire Safety – Since flammability is a potential laser hazard, safeguards must be in place in case of an open flame. An open basin of water should be available in case of laser fire. A fire extinguisher should be present in the room. Patient skin near the laser treatment area should be washed free of cosmetic products that might contain alcohol. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Ablative Laser Resurfacing -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Laser-appropriate protective material (including eye protection) for both patient and healthcare personnel

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1144
retrieved_docs.content: HYDROTHERAPY AND DIATHERMY PROCEDURES are therapeutic modalities that are classified under the broader category of إرحال أيوني, indicating that these water‑based and electromagnetic treatment techniques are considered part of the larger group of ionic‑related therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 148
retrieved_docs.content: methods and classified as a life‑saving therapeutic intent. Physical‑medicine procedures are organized under diagnostic physical therapy, which serves as a parent to other physical‑therapy therapeutic procedures and to electrostimulation therapy. Hydrotherapy and diathermy procedures are classified under ion‑related therapies (إرحال أيوني), connecting them to ion‑based mechanisms and the physical‑medicine branch. Laser interstitial thermal therapy (LITT) under guidance is positioned as a child of both miscellaneous procedures and other or gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures, linking this minimally invasive ablative technique to the broader non‑operative therapeutic category. Miscellaneous procedures are also associated with prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, reinforcing the connection between diverse non‑surgical interventions and preventive care. Unlisted therapeutic procedures, such as irrigation of implanted venous access devices for drug delivery systems, are

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2567
retrieved_docs.content: Laser interstitial thermal therapy (LITT) performed under imaging guidance is classified as an “other or gastrointestinal therapeutic procedure.” This minimally invasive technique uses laser energy to thermally ablate targeted tissue, with real‑time imaging (such as MRI or CT) providing precise navigation and monitoring. By falling within the broader category of other or gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures, LITT is recognized as a specialized treatment option for lesions in gastrointestinal and adjacent structures, offering a focused alternative to more invasive surgical interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1728
retrieved_docs.content: Extracorporeal lithotripsy; urinary is a non‑invasive therapeutic technique used to address the pathology of حَصاة (urinary stones). By delivering focused shock waves to the stone, the procedure fragments the حَصاة into smaller pieces that can be passed spontaneously, thereby reducing the need for more invasive surgical interventions and improving patient outcomes in the management of urolithiasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2454
retrieved_docs.content: Laser interstitial thermal therapy (LITT) performed under imaging guidance is classified as a type of Other miscellaneous procedures, which in turn is a subset of Other nonoperative procedures. In addition to this hierarchical placement, LITT is also considered part of the broader category of other or gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures. This dual categorization reflects that LITT is a non‑surgical, therapeutic intervention that may be applied in gastrointestinal contexts as well as other miscellaneous clinical situations, helping clinicians and coders locate the procedure within comprehensive procedural taxonomies for accurate documentation and appropriate patient care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2052
retrieved_docs.content: ENZYMES FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS are classified as a type of PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS. The broader category PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS includes CICATRICANTS, which are agents that promote scar formation and facilitate wound healing. Consequently, enzymes used in wound and ulcer therapy belong to the larger group of cicatricant preparations, highlighting their role within the spectrum of therapeutic agents aimed at promoting tissue repair.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 849
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy with destruction of tumor by laser surgery is categorized as a destructive surgical procedure and employs a laser‑based method to achieve tumor ablation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 844
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures involving the use of pharmaceuticals encompass a range of therapeutic interventions, and a specific sub‑procedure within this category is the administration of inhaled nitric oxide, which is considered a child procedure of the broader pharmaceutical‑based procedural group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 872
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of antibiotic and laser interstitial thermal therapy (LITT) performed under guidance are specific examples of other miscellaneous procedures. Other miscellaneous procedures are a subset of other nonoperative procedures, indicating that they are therapeutic interventions carried out without surgical incision. In addition, laser interstitial thermal therapy under guidance is also classified under other or gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures, reflecting its broader application in therapeutic contexts beyond the general miscellaneous category. This hierarchical organization clarifies that both injection of antibiotic and LITT are non‑operative, miscellaneous therapeutic techniques, with LITT additionally fitting within the wider group of other or gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures.

Overall, the framework connects urethral reconstructive surgeries, broader plication concepts, and hemorrhage‑control suture plications within a unified system of surgical repair techniques that rely on suturing devices and serve therapeutic, hemostatic, and, in some cases, cosmetic purposes.

This relationship highlights that enzymatic formulations belong to the broader category of wound‑and‑ulcer treatments, emphasizing their role within the spectrum of medical preparations aimed at promoting healing and tissue repair.

Laser interstitial thermal therapy (LITT) under guidance is classified as a child of Other miscellaneous procedures and simultaneously as a child of other or gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures, indicating that LITT is both a miscellaneous non‑operative intervention and a specialized therapeutic technique targeting gastrointestinal or related tissues. Other miscellaneous procedures are further situated under the parent category of Other nonoperative procedures, reinforcing their placement within the spectrum of non‑surgical medical actions. Collectively, these relationships illustrate a structured hierarchy in which Administration – action encompasses various drug and supplement delivery methods; therapeutic intent links specific treatments such as selenium supplementation, calcium supplementation, CPR, and substance‑detox programs

retrieved_docs.content: Preparations for treatment of wounds and ulcers are classified as cicatrants, meaning they are agents that promote tissue repair and scar formation, and are used to enhance the healing process of wounds and ulcerative lesions.
